quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Nuclear notation"?

options.text: Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows
F_b = ρ_fluid · V_displaced · g; an object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force (average density < fluid density)
P = εσAT⁴; total power radiated by a body; P ∝ T⁴ means doubling temperature increases radiation 16-fold
ᴬ_Z X: A = mass number (p + n), Z = atomic number (protons), N = A - Z = neutrons; isotopes differ in N

quiz_questions.explanation: "Nuclear notation" is defined as: ᴬ_Z X: A = mass number (p + n), Z = atomic number (protons), N = A - Z = neutrons; isotopes differ in N. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Strong nuclear force"?

options.text: ε = BLv; induced when a conductor of length L moves with velocity v perpendicular to field B
Binds nucleons; range ≈ 1 fm; much stronger than electromagnetic force at nuclear distances; charge-independent (p-p = p-n = n-n)
Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier
Angular momentum L = nℏ (n = 1,2,3,...); equivalently, nλ = 2πr (standing wave condition for electron orbit)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Strong nuclear force" is defined as: Binds nucleons; range ≈ 1 fm; much stronger than electromagnetic force at nuclear distances; charge-independent (p-p = p-n = n-n). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Nuclear binding energy"?

options.text: 1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ + ...; total voltage = sum of individual voltages; same charge on each capacitor
Energy required to separate all nucleons; comes from mass defect via E = mc²; higher binding energy per nucleon = more stable
k = R/N_A = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K; relates temperature to individual molecular energy
J.J. Thomson used crossed E and B fields to measure the electron's charge-to-mass ratio e/m, discovering the electron

quiz_questions.explanation: "Nuclear binding energy" is defined as: Energy required to separate all nucleons; comes from mass defect via E = mc²; higher binding energy per nucleon = more stable. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mass defect"?

options.text: β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) in decibels (dB); I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² is the threshold of hearing; each 10 dB = 10× intensity
Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted
τ = nIAB sin θ; n = number of turns, I = current, A = loop area, B = field strength, θ = angle between area vector and B
Δm = (Z·m_p + N·m_n) - m_nucleus; the 'missing mass' converted to binding energy; Δm > 0 for all stable nuclei

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mass defect" is defined as: Δm = (Z·m_p + N·m_n) - m_nucleus; the 'missing mass' converted to binding energy; Δm > 0 for all stable nuclei. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Nuclear stability"?

options.text: V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge; scalar quantity measured in volts (V = J/C)
Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted
Stable nuclei lie along the valley of stability in the N vs. Z chart; too many or too few neutrons leads to radioactive decay
ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Nuclear stability" is defined as: Stable nuclei lie along the valley of stability in the N vs. Z chart; too many or too few neutrons leads to radioactive decay. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ᴬ_Z X: A = mass number (p + n), Z = atomic number (protons), N = A - Z = neutrons; isotopes differ in N"?

options.text: Combined power of thin lenses in contact
Thomson's experiment
Reflecting telescope
Nuclear notation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Nuclear notation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Binds nucleons; range ≈ 1 fm; much stronger than electromagnetic force at nuclear distances; charge-independent (p-p = p-n = n-n)"?

options.text: Curie temperature
Strong nuclear force
Average power
Multi-lens systems

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Strong nuclear force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Energy required to separate all nucleons; comes from mass defect via E = mc²; higher binding energy per nucleon = more stable"?

options.text: Photon absorption
Emission spectrum
Gas law special cases
Nuclear binding energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Nuclear binding energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Δm = (Z·m_p + N·m_n) - m_nucleus; the 'missing mass' converted to binding energy; Δm > 0 for all stable nuclei"?

options.text: De Broglie wavelength
Rayleigh criterion
Superconductivity
Mass defect

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mass defect".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Stable nuclei lie along the valley of stability in the N vs. Z chart; too many or too few neutrons leads to radioactive decay"?

options.text: Self-inductance
Absorption spectrum
Nuclear stability
Curie temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Nuclear stability".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Nuclear Physics is accurate?

options.text: Light as a Wave examines the wave nature of light in detail.
EMF can be induced by changing B, changing A, or changing the angle θ between B and the area vector.
Nuclear Physics explores the structure and properties of atomic nuclei.
The resolution limit of optical instruments is set by the Rayleigh criterion.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Nuclear Physics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Nuclear Physics?

options.text: Capacitors and Dielectrics store electrical energy in an electric field.
Like mirrors, the thin lens equation 1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f applies (with sign conventions).
The nucleus contains protons (Z) and neutrons (N), collectively called nucleons (A = Z + N).
Radioactivity and Decay describes the spontaneous transformation of unstable nuclei.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Nuclear Physics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Nuclear Physics?

options.text: Diverging lenses always produce virtual, upright, reduced images.
Total internal reflection occurs when light hits the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle: θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁), and only when going from high-n to low-n medium.
The strong nuclear force binds nucleons together, overcoming electrostatic repulsion between protons.
Sound intensity follows an inverse-square law: I ∝ 1/r².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Nuclear Physics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Nuclear Physics?

options.text: Photons and Quantum Mechanics introduces the quantized nature of light and energy.
Lenses use refraction to form images.
The standing-wave condition (electron wavelength must fit the orbital circumference: nλ = 2πr) explains quantization.
This force is short-range (~1 fm), extremely strong, and charge-independent.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Nuclear Physics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Nuclear Physics?

options.text: Brewster's angle: reflected light is completely polarized when tan θ_B = n₂/n₁.
Electric Potential relates to the potential energy per unit charge in an electric field.
The resolution limit of optical instruments is set by the Rayleigh criterion.
Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio: light stable nuclei have N ≈ Z; heavier nuclei need more neutrons (N > Z).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Nuclear Physics as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Nuclear Physics?

options.text: Corrective lenses: nearsightedness (diverging lens to push focus back) and farsightedness (converging lens to push focus forward) are corrected by choosing the appropriate focal length
Quantum computing chips use superconducting circuits (transmon qubits) cooled to 15 millikelvin—colder than outer space—to maintain quantum coherence for computation
Nuclear medicine: technetium-99m (a metastable nuclear isomer) is the most widely used medical radioisotope, emitting gamma rays for diagnostic imaging of organs
Refrigerators work as reverse heat engines, moving heat from cold interior to warm exterior—they require work input, consistent with the second law

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Nuclear Physics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Nuclear Physics?

options.text: Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts
Tire pressure monitoring: as tires heat up during driving, Gay-Lussac's law predicts pressure increases at constant volume—tire pressure sensors alert drivers to dangerous changes
Noise-canceling headphones use destructive interference: a microphone detects ambient sound, and speakers produce the inverted wave to cancel noise
Thermonuclear weapons use fission to trigger fusion: the fission bomb creates temperatures and pressures needed to fuse hydrogen isotopes, releasing vastly more energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Nuclear Physics.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Nuclear Physics applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Rainbows: sunlight refracts upon entering water droplets, reflects internally, and refracts again upon exiting—dispersion separates colors at different angles
Smoke detectors: americium-241 emits alpha particles that ionize air, creating a current; smoke disrupts the current, triggering the alarm
Electron diffraction microscopy: electrons with de Broglie wavelength ~0.01 nm reveal atomic-scale structures in crystals—confirming matter's wave nature
Mass spectrometry in forensics and drug testing: separating and identifying molecules by mass from blood, urine, or environmental samples

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Nuclear Physics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Nuclear Physics?

options.text: Energy storage: supercapacitors bridge the gap between batteries and conventional capacitors, providing rapid charge/discharge for regenerative braking in electric vehicles
Thermonuclear weapons use fission to trigger fusion: the fission bomb creates temperatures and pressures needed to fuse hydrogen isotopes, releasing vastly more energy
Household wiring: outlets are wired in parallel (each at 120V) with circuit breakers in series (trip at 15-20A) to prevent overheating and fires
Nuclear medicine: technetium-99m (a metastable nuclear isomer) is the most widely used medical radioisotope, emitting gamma rays for diagnostic imaging of organs

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Nuclear Physics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Nuclear Physics in practice?

options.text: Xerox copiers use electrostatic attraction: a charged drum attracts toner particles to form an image, then heat fuses the toner to paper
Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts
Photoelectric solar cells: photon energy (E = hf) must exceed the work function of the material to free electrons and generate current—the particle nature of light in action
PET scans detect positron annihilation: the 1.022 MeV of mass-energy from an electron-positron pair converts to two 0.511 MeV gamma rays traveling in opposite directions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Nuclear Physics.
